sentences.base_language: What do you like to do on weekends?
sentences.target_language: Qu'est-ce que tu aimes faire le week-end ?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Qu'est-ce que** is the standard question structure for 'what' as the object: *qu'est-ce que tu aimes?* (what do you like?).
**Aimer + infinitive** expresses a general preference: *j'aime lire*, *tu aimes danser*.
**Le week-end** (on weekends) uses the definite article for habitual time expressions: *le matin*, *le soir*, *le week-end* — equivalent to English 'in the morning', 'on weekends'.

sentences.base_language: I love reading and going to the cinema.
sentences.target_language: J'adore lire et aller au cinéma.
sentences.grammarNote:
**J'adore + infinitive** (I love doing…) — like *aimer*, *adorer* is followed directly by an infinitive with no preposition.
**Aller au cinéma** (to go to the cinema) — **au** = à + le; *cinéma* is masculine.
For activities, French uses infinitives where English uses -ing gerunds: *lire* (reading), *aller* (going).

sentences.base_language: I also play tennis with a friend on Saturday mornings.
sentences.target_language: Je joue aussi au tennis avec un ami le samedi matin.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Jouer au + sport** is used for sports played against an opponent or in a team: *jouer au tennis*, *jouer au football*, *jouer aux échecs*.
**Au** = à + le — tennis is masculine, so *à + le tennis* = **au tennis**.
**Le samedi matin** (on Saturday mornings) — the singular definite article expresses a habitual/repeated action, equivalent to 'every Saturday morning'.

sentences.base_language: That sounds great. Do you play well?
sentences.grammarNote:
**C'est sympa** — *sympa* (nice/fun) is an invariable informal adjective, short for *sympathique*. It does not change for gender or number.
The question **tu joues bien ?** uses rising intonation — the most common question form in casual spoken French.
**Bien** (well) is an adverb placed after the conjugated verb: *tu joues bien*, *il parle bien français*.

**Pas vraiment** (not really) — a partial negation without *ne* in informal speech. In full written French: *je ne joue pas vraiment bien*.
**C'est + adjective** is the standard structure for 'it is + adjective' in French: *c'est amusant*, *c'est difficile*, *c'est beau*.
**Amusant** (fun/amusing) is the adjective form; the verb is *amuser* (to amuse) — compare *s'amuser* (to have fun, reflexive).

sentences.base_language: And you? What are your hobbies?
sentences.target_language: Et toi ? Quels sont tes passe-temps ?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Toi** is a **stressed pronoun** (pronom tonique) — used after prepositions and in short answers or contrasts: *et toi ?*, *c'est pour toi*, *moi aussi*.
**Quels** is the masculine plural interrogative adjective (what/which) — it agrees with *passe-temps*: *quel* (m.s.), *quelle* (f.s.), *quels* (m.pl.), *quelles* (f.pl.).
**Passe-temps** (hobbies/pastimes) is invariable — it looks the same in singular and plural.

sentences.base_language: I cook a lot. I love trying new recipes.
sentences.target_language: Je cuisine beaucoup. J'adore essayer de nouvelles recettes.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Cuisiner** (to cook) is a regular -er verb: je cuisine, tu cuisines, il cuisine.
**De nouvelles recettes** — the indefinite article **des** becomes **de** before an adjective that precedes the noun: *de nouvelles recettes*, *de beaux paysages*, *de bonnes idées*.
**Essayer de + infinitive** means 'to try to do': *j'essaie de cuisiner*, *j'essaie de comprendre*.

sentences.base_language: I also paint when I have free time.
sentences.target_language: Je peins aussi quand j'ai du temps libre.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Peindre** (to paint) is an irregular -re verb: je **peins**, tu peins, il peint, nous peignons, vous peignez, ils peignent.
**Du temps libre** uses the partitive article **du** (= de + le) — *temps* is masculine and uncountable here: *du temps*, *du courage*, *du travail*.
**Quand** (when) introduces a time clause; unlike English 'when', it can also mean 'whenever' for habitual actions.

**Waouh** is the French spelling of the exclamation 'wow' — also sometimes written *waow* or *ouah*.
**C'est impressionnant** — **impressionnant** is an adjective derived from the present participle of *impressionner* (to impress). Many French adjectives end in **-ant**: *amusant*, *intéressant*, *étonnant*.
These adjectives are invariable in the predicate position after *c'est*.
